headline: Chip Reopen Sorts Winners Monday
deck: Equity futures pointed higher into the first US cash session since Thursday’s chip rout, with dispersion across semiconductor names setting the tone. Rotation inside the AI trade, a coin-flip September hike path, and oil’s split crude-versus-products story frame a mid-session tape that carries no full-day closes yet.
body: Monday’s first US cash session since Thursday’s semiconductor selloff opened in the first hours with equity futures pointing higher, a mid-session read that carries no full-day closes yet. The PHLX Semiconductor Index had finished at 12,626.22 on 2 July, down 5.44%, and that print set the baseline for the reopen [E2]. Dispersion across chip-linked names widened from the first premarket quotes, with contract winners drawing bids while capital-intensive issuers faced selling pressure. Contract headlines and financing headlines moved individual prices before the cash bell rang.

Broadcom rose about 4% in premarket trading after a company filing disclosed an extension of its custom-ASIC supply partnership with Apple through 2031 [E3]. SK Hynix filed a $28.07 billion US ADR offering with the SEC on the same morning, and its Seoul-listed shares fell 3.4% on the deal’s footprint in first-hour trade [E4]. The pairing showed how supply-contract news and equity-financing news could push neighboring semiconductor names in opposite directions on the same reopen. Neither move repriced the entire complex uniformly.

Morgan Stanley told clients that AI-related gains may pivot from chipmakers toward hyperscalers as the cycle matures [E5]. Goldman’s flow data showed hedge funds selling chip stocks for a fourth consecutive week [E6]. Capital appeared to rotate across layers of the AI stack while thematic exposure to compute held firm. Rotation inside the AI trade framed Monday’s semiconductor action as funds shifted among chip suppliers, cloud operators, and financing-heavy issuers.

June nonfarm payrolls rose 57,000, with prior months revised lower and unemployment at 4.2%, a soft print that repriced the rate path [E1]. CME’s FedWatch tool placed September hike odds near a coin flip as of Monday’s first hours [E7]. Wednesday’s FOMC minutes stand as the week’s pivot event for anyone mapping cuts versus hikes into autumn. The payroll miss and the reopen’s risk appetite shared one tape without resolving whether soft labour data or chip dispersion would dominate the week’s close.

OPEC+ confirmed another 188,000 barrels per day of supply for August, the fifth straight monthly increase [E10]. Crude pressed lower into the reopen on that headline while refined-product crack spreads sat at their highest since 2022 according to widely followed commodity commentary [E11]. Glut conditions in crude ran alongside tightness in products, a split that kept the barrel’s message ambiguous at the Monday open. The Brent sub-$70 settle-by-Friday call stays YES at p=0.55, and first-hour trade moved further in its favour without settling the question. No barrel close yet exists to confirm the call either way.

USD/JPY traded around 162.3 in Monday’s first hours, within twenty pips of the 162.50 trigger, another mid-session read without a New York close. Leveraged funds held roughly 97,092 net short yen contracts in CFTC data through 23 June [E8]. Japan’s Ministry of Finance disclosed zero intervention for the 28 May–26 June window [E9]. Crowded shorts against a silent ministry describe a setup that can resolve suddenly once officials choose to act or funds choose to cover.

The open USD/JPY call stays NO with the posterior at p=0.38 that the pair prints 162.50 by the 10 July New York close, up from p=0.25 when the call opened. Monday’s proximity to the trigger moved the probability without flipping the side. A print at 162.50 would register as a miss on the record for that open call. The yen leg and the Brent leg both shifted on Monday, yet neither forecast met its resolution deadline in the first hours of trade.

evidence_box.source: Ole S. Hansen, Saxo head of commodity strategy, on X
